items.title: How head and neck cancers are defined and where they arise
items.content:
Head and neck cancer is predominantly a squamous-cell, mucosal disease.
Epithelial carcinomas of the head and neck arise from the mucosal surfaces and are typically squamous cell in origin, including tumors of the paranasal sinuses, oral cavity, nasopharynx, oropharynx, hypopharynx, and larynx.
In North America and Europe these tumors usually arise from the oral cavity, oropharynx, or larynx, while nasopharyngeal cancer is endemic in East Asia and some Mediterranean countries.
Salivary gland tumors differ in etiology, histopathology, presentation, and therapy (and are rare and heterogeneous), and thyroid malignancies are considered separately.
keyPoints: Head and neck cancers are mostly squamous cell carcinomas of mucosal surfaces
Sites: paranasal sinuses, oral cavity, nasopharynx, oropharynx, hypopharynx, larynx
Nasopharyngeal cancer is endemic in East Asia; salivary and thyroid tumors are distinct
items.reference: Head and Neck Cancer: "Epithelial carcinomas of the head and neck arise from the mucosal surfaces in the head and neck and typically are squamous cell in origin."

items.title: Why alcohol and tobacco act synergistically in head and neck cancer
items.content:
The two dominant environmental risk factors compound each other.
Alcohol and tobacco use are the most significant environmental risk factors for head and neck cancer, and when used together they act synergistically.
Smokeless tobacco is an etiologic agent for oral cancers, and other potential carcinogens include marijuana and occupational exposures (nickel refining, textile fibers, woodworking).
Dietary factors may also contribute — incidence is higher with the lowest fruit and vegetable consumption, and retinoid supplements (cis-retinoic acid) have not been shown to prevent head and neck cancer and may increase risk in active smokers.
keyPoints: Alcohol + tobacco are the major risk factors and act synergistically together
Smokeless tobacco causes oral cancer; marijuana and occupational exposures contribute
Retinoid supplements do not prevent HNC and may raise risk in active smokers
items.reference: ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS: "Alcohol and tobacco use are the most significant environmental risk factors for head and neck cancer, and when used together, they act synergistically."

items.title: How HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer differs from tobacco-related disease
items.content:
HPV has created a distinct, growing subtype of head and neck cancer with a better outlook.
In Western countries HPV is associated with a rising incidence of oropharyngeal tumors (tonsillar bed and tongue base) — over 50% of US oropharyngeal tumors are HPV-caused (HPV-16 dominant) — even as alcohol/tobacco-related cancers decline.
HPV-related cancer occurs in younger patients (forties to fifties), is associated with more sexual partners and oral sexual practices, frequently presents with neck lymphadenopathy as the first sign, and carries a better prognosis (especially in nonsmokers).
The nine-valent HPV vaccine may prevent the disease but is unlikely to lower incidence for decades due to the long latency.
keyPoints: >50% of US oropharyngeal cancers are HPV-related (HPV-16 dominant); incidence rising
Younger patients; often presents with neck lymphadenopathy; better prognosis (esp. nonsmokers)
HPV vaccine may prevent disease but won't reduce incidence for decades (long latency)
items.reference: ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS: "Over 50% of oropharyngeal tumors are caused by HPV in the United States...HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer occurs in a younger patient population...It is associated with a better prognosis, especially for nonsmokers."

items.title: Why EBV is linked to nasopharyngeal cancer
items.content:
A second virus defines a geographically distinct head and neck cancer.
Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection is frequently associated with nasopharyngeal cancer, especially in endemic areas (East Asia, some Mediterranean countries), and EBV antibody titers can screen high-risk populations and are being studied to monitor treatment response.
Nasopharyngeal cancer has also been associated with salted-fish consumption and indoor pollution.
Histologically, the EBV-associated nonkeratinizing/undifferentiated carcinoma (lymphoepithelioma) contains infiltrating lymphocytes; EBV-related nasopharyngeal cancer can occur at all ages, including teenagers.
keyPoints: EBV is associated with nasopharyngeal cancer (endemic East Asia/Mediterranean)
EBV antibody titers can screen high-risk populations and monitor treatment
Lymphoepithelioma (undifferentiated, lymphocyte-infiltrated) is the EBV-associated histology; occurs at all ages
items.reference: ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS: "Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection is frequently associated with nasopharyngeal cancer, especially in endemic areas."

items.title: How HPV oncogenes initiate head and neck carcinogenesis
items.content:
HPV drives cancer by disabling the same tumor suppressors mutated in carcinogen-induced disease.
HPV oncogenes act through direct inhibition of the p53 and RB tumor-suppressor genes, thereby initiating the carcinogenic process — so HPV-positive tumors achieve via viral proteins what HPV-negative tumors achieve via p53 mutation.
In HPV-negative tumors, p53 mutations are frequent along with affected mitotic signaling, Notch, and cell-cycle pathways.
While overall mutation rates are similar, HPV-positive tumors have a distinct mutational signature with frequent PI3K-pathway alteration; HRAS mutations occur in 4–8% of recurrent disease and may be targetable in a small subset.
keyPoints: HPV oncogenes (E6/E7) directly inhibit p53 and RB to initiate carcinogenesis
HPV-negative tumors: frequent p53 mutations plus mitotic/Notch/cell-cycle pathway changes
HPV-positive tumors have a distinct signature with frequent PI3K-pathway alteration
items.reference: HISTOPATHOLOGY, CARCINOGENESIS, AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY: "HPV oncogenes act through direct inhibition of the p53 and RB tumor-suppressor genes, thereby initiating the carcinogenic process."

items.title: Why treated early head and neck cancer patients risk dying from second primaries
items.content:
Whole-mucosa carcinogen exposure creates a field at risk for new cancers.
Because the entire pharyngeal mucosa is exposed to alcohol- and tobacco-related carcinogens, multiple synchronous or metachronous cancers can occur; over time, patients with treated early-stage tobacco/alcohol-related head and neck cancer are at greater risk of dying from a second malignancy than from recurrence of the primary.
These second primaries are usually not therapy-induced but reflect the same carcinogen exposure, developing in the head and neck, lung, or esophagus.
Therefore CT screening for lung cancer in heavy smokers who have already developed a head and neck cancer is recommended.
keyPoints: Field cancerization: the whole upper-aerodigestive mucosa is at risk → second primaries
Treated early HNC patients die more from second malignancies than from recurrence
Second primaries arise in head/neck, lung, or esophagus → CT lung screening in heavy smokers
items.reference: HISTOPATHOLOGY, CARCINOGENESIS, AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY: "patients with treated early-stage tobacco- and alcohol-related head and neck cancer are at greater risk of dying from a second malignancy than from a recurrence of the primary disease."

items.title: How head and neck cancer presents by primary site
items.content:
Symptoms are site-specific and often subtle early.
Nasopharyngeal cancer typically causes no early symptoms but may produce unilateral serous otitis media (eustachian tube obstruction), nasal obstruction, or epistaxis, with cranial neuropathies in advanced skull-base disease.
Oral cavity carcinomas present as non-healing ulcers, changes in denture fit, or painful masses; tongue-base/oropharyngeal tumors cause decreased tongue mobility and speech change, and HPV-related tumors frequently present with neck lymphadenopathy.
Hoarseness may be an early symptom of laryngeal cancer, and persistent hoarseness requires specialist referral for laryngoscopy.
keyPoints: Nasopharynx: unilateral serous otitis media (eustachian obstruction), nasal obstruction, epistaxis, cranial neuropathies
Oral cavity: non-healing ulcer, denture-fit change; tongue base/oropharynx: speech change, neck nodes (HPV)
Larynx: hoarseness (an early symptom) → specialist referral if persistent
items.reference: CLINICAL PRESENTATION: "Hoarseness may be an early symptom of laryngeal cancer, and persistent hoarseness requires referral to a specialist for indirect laryngoscopy and/or radiographic studies."

items.title: Why a non-resolving head and neck lesion must not just be treated with more antibiotics
items.content:
Persistent symptoms after empirical antibiotics are a red flag for malignancy.
Patients with nonspecific head and neck signs or symptoms should be evaluated with a thorough otolaryngologic examination, particularly if symptoms persist longer than 2–4 weeks.
If a head and neck lesion treated initially with antibiotics does not resolve in a short period, further workup is indicated — to simply continue the antibiotic treatment may be to lose the chance of early diagnosis of a malignancy.
Leukoplakia (white patch) and erythroplakia (red patch) are premalignant lesions (hyperplasia, dysplasia, or carcinoma in situ) that require biopsy.
keyPoints: Evaluate persistent (>2–4 weeks) head/neck symptoms with otolaryngologic exam
A non-resolving lesion on antibiotics needs workup — continuing antibiotics risks missing cancer
Leukoplakia/erythroplakia are premalignant and require biopsy
items.reference: CLINICAL PRESENTATION: "to simply continue the antibiotic treatment may be to lose the chance of early diagnosis of a malignancy."

items.title: How a cervical node with squamous carcinoma and no obvious primary is worked up
items.content:
An enlarged neck node may be the only sign of an occult head and neck primary.
When enlarged nodes contain squamous cell carcinoma and no primary is evident, the diagnosis is made by fine-needle aspiration or lymph node excision (especially if a single node is involved).
If squamous carcinoma is confirmed, panendoscopy is performed with biopsy of suspicious areas and directed biopsies of common primary sites — nasopharynx, tonsil, tongue base, and pyriform sinus — and tonsillectomy with tongue-base biopsies can reveal a small, often HPV-related primary.
Upper-neck squamous nodes usually indicate a head and neck mucosal primary, whereas supraclavicular nodes may arise from a chest or abdominal primary.
keyPoints: Diagnose neck node by FNA or excision; then panendoscopy with directed biopsies
Sample nasopharynx, tonsil, tongue base, pyriform sinus (HPV primaries are often small)
Upper-neck squamous nodes → head/neck primary; supraclavicular nodes → chest/abdomen primary
items.reference: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: "a panendoscopy should be performed, with biopsy of all suspicious-appearing areas and directed biopsies of common primary sites, such as the nasopharynx, tonsil, tongue base, and pyriform sinus."

items.title: Why HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer has its own staging system
items.content:
Staging reflects the markedly better prognosis of HPV-positive disease.
Head and neck tumors are classified by the AJCC TNM system (T1–T3 by increasing size, T4 usually invasion of bone/muscle/tongue root; nodes staged by size, number, and laterality); overt distant metastases are present in <10% at diagnosis (more common with advanced nodal disease).
Because HPV-related oropharyngeal malignancies consistently have a better prognosis, the eighth-edition AJCC manual includes a separate staging system for them in which patients with advanced nodal stage can still be classified as the equivalent of overall early stage (and a correspondingly good prognosis).
HPV testing should be performed for all newly diagnosed oropharyngeal tumors.
keyPoints: AJCC TNM: T1–T3 by size, T4 = invasion; nodes by size/number/laterality; distant mets <10% at diagnosis
HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer has a separate, more favorable staging system (8th edition)
Test all newly diagnosed oropharyngeal tumors for HPV
items.reference: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: "in the eighth edition of the AJCC staging manual, a separate staging system that takes into account the more favorable outlook of these patients has been included."

items.title: How localized (stage I/II) head and neck cancer is treated
items.content:
Early disease is curable with a single local modality, chosen by site.
Nearly one-third of patients have localized disease (T1/T2, stage I/II without nodal involvement or metastases), treated with curative intent by either surgery or radiation therapy, with the choice depending on anatomic location and institutional expertise.
Radiation is often preferred for laryngeal cancer to preserve voice function, while surgery is preferred for small oral-cavity lesions to avoid long-term radiation complications (xerostomia, osteoradionecrosis, dental decay).
A prophylactic staging neck dissection should be part of the surgical procedure to eliminate occult nodal disease; overall 5-year survival is 60–90%, and most recurrences occur within 2 years and are usually local.
keyPoints: Stage I/II: surgery OR radiation with curative intent (~1/3 of patients); 5-year survival 60–90%
Larynx → radiation (voice preservation); small oral cavity → surgery (avoid radiation toxicity)
Include prophylactic neck dissection surgically; most recurrences are local, within 2 years
items.reference: LOCALIZED DISEASE: "These patients are treated with curative intent by either surgery or radiation therapy."

items.title: How locally/regionally advanced disease is treated with combined modality therapy
items.content:
Advanced disease — the most common presentation — requires more than one modality.
Locally or regionally advanced disease (large primary and/or cervical node metastases) is the stage of presentation for >50% of patients and is treated with curative intent but not usually with surgery or radiation alone.
Combined-modality therapy — surgery and/or radiation plus chemotherapy — is most successful; chemotherapy may be given as induction (before local therapy) or as concomitant (simultaneous) chemoradiotherapy, the latter most commonly used and best supported by evidence.
Five-year survival exceeds 50% in many trials (partly reflecting more HPV-related tumors).
keyPoints: >50% present with locally/regionally advanced disease; treat with combined modality, not single-modality
Chemotherapy is given as induction or concomitant chemoradiotherapy (concomitant is most used/evidence-based)
5-year survival exceeds 50% in many trials
items.reference: LOCALLY OR REGIONALLY ADVANCED DISEASE: "Combined-modality therapy, including surgery and/or radiation therapy and chemotherapy, is most successful."

items.title: Why concomitant chemoradiotherapy improves outcomes despite more toxicity
items.content:
Giving chemotherapy and radiation together enhances local tumor kill where recurrences arise.
Because head and neck recurrences develop most commonly locoregionally, the concomitant approach enhances radiation tumor-cell killing in the presence of chemotherapy (radiation enhancement) and is attractive for bulky tumors.
Toxicity is increased (grade 3/4 mucositis in 70–80%), but meta-analyses document an 8% improvement in 5-year survival with concomitant chemoradiotherapy, and cisplatin is preferentially given weekly during a 6–7 week course of daily radiotherapy.
Concomitant chemoradiotherapy also produces better laryngectomy-free (organ-preserving) survival than radiation alone in advanced larynx cancer.
keyPoints: Concomitant chemoradiotherapy enhances local kill (where recurrences occur) — 8% 5-year survival gain
Toxicity is higher (grade 3/4 mucositis 70–80%); cisplatin given weekly during radiotherapy
Improves laryngectomy-free (organ-preserving) survival in advanced larynx cancer
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "meta-analyses of randomized trials document an improvement in 5-year survival of 8% with concomitant chemotherapy and radiation therapy."

items.title: How induction chemotherapy enables organ preservation
items.content:
Sequential chemotherapy before local therapy can preserve organs and improve cure.
In the induction strategy, patients receive chemotherapy before surgery and radiation — the current standard is the three-drug regimen of docetaxel, cisplatin, and fluorouracil (TPF/DCF).
Most patients who receive three cycles show tumor reduction, with clinically complete responses in up to half.
This sequential multimodality therapy allows organ preservation in laryngeal and hypopharyngeal cancer and yields higher cure rates than radiotherapy alone; for advanced nasopharyngeal cancer, neoadjuvant chemotherapy before concomitant chemoradiotherapy is now standard, with 5-year survival exceeding 80% in a Southeast Asian study.
keyPoints: Induction regimen = docetaxel + cisplatin + 5-FU (TPF), before surgery/radiation
Enables organ preservation (larynx, hypopharynx); higher cure than radiotherapy alone
Advanced nasopharyngeal cancer: neoadjuvant chemo before chemoradiotherapy is standard (5-yr survival >80%)
items.reference: Induction Chemotherapy: "current standard is a three-drug regimen of docetaxel, cisplatin, and fluorouracil [5-FU]...This 'sequential' multimodality therapy allows for organ preservation in patients with laryngeal and hypopharyngeal cancer..."

items.title: Why cisplatin (not cetuximab) is the radiosensitizer in HPV-related cancer
items.content:
Substituting cetuximab to reduce toxicity backfired in HPV-positive disease.
HPV-related cancers have a favorable outcome following cisplatin-based chemoradiotherapy, but trials substituting the EGFR inhibitor cetuximab for cisplatin in this population showed inferior survival — so cisplatin remains the standard radiosensitizer.
Similarly, investigation of immune checkpoint inhibitors in this setting has not yet improved outcomes.
Cetuximab does increase survival versus radiotherapy alone (an option for patients who cannot tolerate concurrent chemotherapy), but adding cetuximab to standard chemoradiotherapy has not further improved survival and is not recommended.
keyPoints: In HPV-related cancer, replacing cisplatin with cetuximab gives inferior survival — keep cisplatin
Checkpoint inhibitors have not yet improved outcomes in this setting
Cetuximab beats radiotherapy alone (for cisplatin-ineligible patients) but adds nothing to chemoradiotherapy
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "trials substituting cisplatin with the EGFR inhibitor cetuximab in that patient population have shown inferior survival."

items.title: How postoperative chemoradiotherapy is selected after surgery
items.content:
High-risk pathology after surgery upgrades adjuvant therapy from radiation to chemoradiotherapy.
The success of concomitant chemoradiotherapy in unresectable disease led to its use in resected intermediate-stage disease as postoperative therapy.
Concomitant chemoradiotherapy produces a significant improvement over postoperative radiation alone for tumors with higher-risk features — extracapsular spread beyond involved lymph nodes, involvement of multiple lymph nodes, or positive margins at the primary site.
In intermediate-stage tumors (stage III/early IV), concomitant chemoradiotherapy can also be primary treatment for unresectable disease or an organ-preserving approach (especially for laryngeal cancer).
keyPoints: Postoperative chemoradiotherapy beats radiation alone for high-risk pathology
High-risk features: extracapsular spread, multiple involved nodes, positive primary margins
Also used as primary therapy for unresectable disease or larynx organ preservation
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "Concomitant chemoradiotherapy produces a significant improvement over postoperative radiation therapy alone for patients whose tumors demonstrate higher risk features, such as extracapsular spread...involvement of multiple lymph nodes, or positive margins..."

items.title: Why HPV-related cancers are candidates for treatment de-escalation
items.content:
The excellent prognosis of HPV-positive disease motivates reducing long-term toxicity.
Given the high survival rates of advanced HPV-related oropharyngeal tumors with combined-modality therapy, de-escalation protocols aim to decrease long-term morbidity from high-dose radiation — extensive neck fibrosis, swallowing problems, and osteoradionecrosis of the jaw.
Studies are investigating lower radiation doses, induction chemotherapy with subsequent omission/reduction of chemoradiation in very good responders, and robotic surgery for better tongue-base/tonsil visualization.
Importantly, many patients with multi-node disease still require postoperative chemoradiotherapy (negating de-escalation), so current guidelines treat HPV-related and carcinogen-induced tumors identically pending validation by ongoing trials.
keyPoints: De-escalation goal: cut long-term toxicity (neck fibrosis, dysphagia, osteoradionecrosis) in HPV-positive disease
Strategies: lower radiation dose, response-adapted chemoradiation, robotic surgery
Not yet standard — current guidelines treat HPV-positive and carcinogen-induced tumors identically
items.reference: TREATMENT APPROACHES FOR HPV-RELATED HEAD AND NECK CANCERS: "At present, treatment guidelines for HPV-related tumors are identical to carcinogen-induced tumors."

items.title: How recurrent and/or metastatic head and neck cancer is managed
items.content:
Recurrence is common and usually treated palliatively.
Five to 10% of patients present with metastatic disease, and 30–50% of those with locoregionally advanced disease experience recurrence, frequently outside the head and neck region.
Patients with recurrent and/or metastatic disease are, with few exceptions, treated with palliative intent.
Some patients may require local or regional radiation therapy for pain control, but most are given systemic therapy.
keyPoints: 5–10% present with metastases; 30–50% of locoregionally advanced disease recurs
Recurrent/metastatic disease is usually treated with palliative intent
Local radiation for pain control; most receive systemic therapy
items.reference: RECURRENT AND/OR METASTATIC DISEASE: "Patients with recurrent and/or metastatic disease are, with few exceptions, treated with palliative intent."

items.title: How salivary gland tumors differ by gland and histology
items.content:
Salivary gland tumors are a distinct, heterogeneous group with a gland-dependent risk of malignancy.
They arise from major (parotid, submandibular, sublingual) or minor salivary glands; most parotid tumors are benign, but about half of submandibular and sublingual gland tumors and most minor salivary gland tumors are malignant.
Malignant types include mucoepidermoid and adenoid cystic carcinomas and adenocarcinomas.
No specific risk factors or environmental carcinogens have been identified for salivary gland tumors.
keyPoints: Parotid tumors mostly benign; ~50% of submandibular/sublingual and most minor-gland tumors are malignant
Malignant histologies: mucoepidermoid carcinoma, adenoid cystic carcinoma, adenocarcinoma
No specific risk factor/carcinogen identified for salivary gland tumors
items.reference: HISTOPATHOLOGY, CARCINOGENESIS, AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY: "Most parotid tumors are benign, but half of submandibular and sublingual gland tumors and most minor salivary gland tumors are malignant."

items.title: Why EGFR overexpression does not predict response to EGFR inhibitors
items.content:
A common molecular marker proved disappointing as a predictive biomarker.
Early reports demonstrated frequent overexpression of the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) in head and neck cancer, and overexpression correlated with poor prognosis.
However, EGFR overexpression has not been shown to be a good predictor of tumor response to EGFR inhibitors, which are active in only about 10–15% of patients as single agents.
This dissociation between expression and response illustrates that target overexpression alone does not guarantee dependence on that target for the tumor's growth.
keyPoints: EGFR is frequently overexpressed in head and neck cancer and correlates with poor prognosis
EGFR overexpression does NOT predict response to EGFR inhibitors (active in only ~10–15% as single agents)
Target overexpression does not equal target dependence
items.reference: HISTOPATHOLOGY, CARCINOGENESIS, AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY: "it has not been to be a good predictor of tumor response to EGFR inhibitors, which are active in only about 10–15% of patients as single agents."

items.question: Most epithelial carcinomas of the head and neck (oral cavity, pharynx, larynx) are of which histologic type?
items.explanation: Epithelial carcinomas of the head and neck arise from mucosal surfaces and are typically squamous cell in origin. Salivary gland tumors are a distinct, heterogeneous group.
items.reference: Head and Neck Cancer: "Epithelial carcinomas of the head and neck arise from the mucosal surfaces in the head and neck and typically are squamous cell in origin."

items.question: What is true of alcohol and tobacco as risk factors for head and neck cancer?
options: Only tobacco is a risk factor
They act synergistically when used together
Neither is a significant risk factor
items.explanation: Alcohol and tobacco are the most significant environmental risk factors, and when used together they act synergistically.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS: "Alcohol and tobacco use are the most significant environmental risk factors for head and neck cancer, and when used together, they act synergistically."

items.question: A 48-year-old nonsmoker presents with neck lymphadenopathy and a base-of-tongue tumor that is HPV-positive. Compared with tobacco-related cancer, this tumor is associated with:
items.explanation: HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer occurs in younger patients (forties to fifties), often presents with neck lymphadenopathy, and carries a better prognosis, especially in nonsmokers.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS: "HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer occurs in a younger patient population...It is associated with a better prognosis, especially for nonsmokers."

items.question: Which HPV subtype is the dominant cause of HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer?
items.explanation: HPV-16 is the dominant viral subtype in HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer, though HPV-18 and other oncogenic subtypes are also seen; over 50% of US oropharyngeal tumors are HPV-caused.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS: "HPV-16 is the dominant viral subtype, although HPV-18 and other oncogenic subtypes are seen as well."

items.question: Which virus is frequently associated with nasopharyngeal carcinoma, especially in endemic areas?
items.explanation: Epstein-Barr virus is frequently associated with nasopharyngeal cancer, especially in endemic areas; EBV antibody titers can screen high-risk populations and are being studied to monitor treatment.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS: "Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection is frequently associated with nasopharyngeal cancer, especially in endemic areas."

items.question: HPV initiates head and neck carcinogenesis primarily by:
options: Directly inhibiting the p53 and RB tumor-suppressor genes
items.explanation: HPV oncogenes act through direct inhibition of the p53 and RB tumor-suppressor genes, initiating carcinogenesis — achieving via viral proteins what HPV-negative tumors achieve through p53 mutation.
items.reference: HISTOPATHOLOGY, CARCINOGENESIS, AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY: "HPV oncogenes act through direct inhibition of the p53 and RB tumor-suppressor genes, thereby initiating the carcinogenic process."

items.question: Over long-term follow-up, a patient cured of an early tobacco/alcohol-related head and neck cancer is at greatest risk of dying from:
items.explanation: Because of carcinogen exposure of the entire upper-aerodigestive mucosa (field cancerization), treated early-stage patients are at greater risk of dying from a second malignancy than from recurrence; second primaries arise in the head/neck, lung, or esophagus, justifying lung CT screening.
items.reference: HISTOPATHOLOGY, CARCINOGENESIS, AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY: "patients with treated early-stage tobacco- and alcohol-related head and neck cancer are at greater risk of dying from a second malignancy than from a recurrence of the primary disease."

items.question: A new unilateral serous otitis media in an adult should prompt evaluation for a tumor at which site?
items.explanation: Nasopharyngeal cancer may cause unilateral serous otitis media due to eustachian tube obstruction (as well as nasal obstruction or epistaxis); advanced disease causes cranial neuropathies from skull-base involvement.
items.reference: CLINICAL PRESENTATION: "it may cause unilateral serous otitis media due to obstruction of the eustachian tube..."

items.question: A persistent oropharyngeal lesion does not resolve after a course of antibiotics. What is the appropriate next step?
options: Proceed with further workup (specialist evaluation and biopsy)
Switch to a different antibiotic and recheck in 3 months
items.explanation: If a head and neck lesion treated with antibiotics does not resolve in a short period, further workup is indicated; simply continuing antibiotics risks losing the chance for early cancer diagnosis.
items.reference: CLINICAL PRESENTATION: "If a head and neck lesion treated initially with antibiotics does not resolve in a short period, further workup is indicated..."

items.question: A patient has an upper-neck lymph node with squamous cell carcinoma but no visible primary. Where should directed biopsies be taken during panendoscopy?
options: Nasopharynx, tonsil, tongue base, and pyriform sinus
items.explanation: When squamous carcinoma is found in a neck node with no visible primary, panendoscopy with directed biopsies of common sites — nasopharynx, tonsil, tongue base, and pyriform sinus — is performed (HPV primaries are often small).
items.reference: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: "directed biopsies of common primary sites, such as the nasopharynx, tonsil, tongue base, and pyriform sinus."

items.question: For a small early-stage laryngeal cancer, which treatment is often preferred and why?
options: Radiation therapy, to preserve voice function
items.explanation: Radiation therapy is often preferred for laryngeal cancer to preserve voice function; surgery is preferred for small oral-cavity lesions to avoid long-term radiation complications (xerostomia, osteoradionecrosis, dental decay).
items.reference: LOCALIZED DISEASE: "Radiation therapy is often preferred for laryngeal cancer to preserve voice function..."

items.question: Which treatment approach is most commonly used and best supported by evidence for locally advanced head and neck cancer?
items.explanation: Locally/regionally advanced disease is treated with combined modality therapy; concomitant chemoradiotherapy is the most commonly used approach and is supported by the best evidence (8% 5-year survival improvement).
items.reference: LOCALLY OR REGIONALLY ADVANCED DISEASE: "The latter [concomitant chemoradiotherapy] is most commonly used and supported by the best evidence."

items.question: By approximately how much does concomitant chemoradiotherapy improve 5-year survival over radiation alone, per meta-analyses?
items.explanation: Meta-analyses document an improvement in 5-year survival of about 8% with concomitant chemoradiotherapy, at the cost of increased toxicity (grade 3/4 mucositis in 70–80%). Cisplatin is given weekly during radiotherapy.
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "meta-analyses of randomized trials document an improvement in 5-year survival of 8% with concomitant chemotherapy and radiation therapy."

items.question: The current standard induction chemotherapy regimen for head and neck cancer is:
items.explanation: The current standard induction regimen is the three-drug combination of docetaxel, cisplatin, and fluorouracil (TPF/DCF), which enables organ preservation in laryngeal and hypopharyngeal cancer.
items.reference: Induction Chemotherapy: "current standard is a three-drug regimen of docetaxel, cisplatin, and fluorouracil [5-FU]..."

items.question: In HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer, trials substituting cetuximab for cisplatin during chemoradiotherapy showed:
items.explanation: Trials substituting the EGFR inhibitor cetuximab for cisplatin in HPV-related cancer showed inferior survival, so cisplatin remains the standard radiosensitizer in this population.
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "trials substituting cisplatin with the EGFR inhibitor cetuximab in that patient population have shown inferior survival."

items.question: Postoperative concomitant chemoradiotherapy (rather than radiation alone) is indicated for resected head and neck cancer with which high-risk feature?
options: Extracapsular nodal spread or positive primary margins
Single small lymph node without spread
items.explanation: Postoperative chemoradiotherapy improves over radiation alone for tumors with high-risk features such as extracapsular spread beyond involved nodes, multiple involved nodes, or positive margins at the primary site.
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "such as extracapsular spread beyond involved lymph nodes, involvement of multiple lymph nodes, or positive margins at the primary site following surgery."

items.question: EGFR inhibitors are active as single agents in approximately what fraction of head and neck cancer patients, illustrating that EGFR overexpression does not predict response?
items.explanation: EGFR overexpression is common and correlates with poor prognosis but does not predict response to EGFR inhibitors, which are active in only about 10–15% of patients as single agents.
items.reference: HISTOPATHOLOGY, CARCINOGENESIS, AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY: "EGFR inhibitors, which are active in only about 10–15% of patients as single agents."

items.question: Which statement about salivary gland tumors is correct?
options: Most parotid tumors are benign, but most minor salivary gland tumors are malignant
All salivary gland tumors are benign
Minor salivary gland tumors are always benign
items.explanation: Most parotid tumors are benign, but about half of submandibular/sublingual gland tumors and most minor salivary gland tumors are malignant. Malignant types include mucoepidermoid and adenoid cystic carcinomas and adenocarcinomas.
items.reference: HISTOPATHOLOGY, CARCINOGENESIS, AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY: "Most parotid tumors are benign, but half of submandibular and sublingual gland tumors and most minor salivary gland tumors are malignant."

items.question: Patients with recurrent and/or metastatic head and neck cancer are generally treated with:
items.explanation: Patients with recurrent and/or metastatic disease are, with few exceptions, treated with palliative intent — most receive systemic therapy, with local radiation for pain control in some.
items.reference: RECURRENT AND/OR METASTATIC DISEASE: "Patients with recurrent and/or metastatic disease are, with few exceptions, treated with palliative intent."

items.question: What is the rationale for investigating treatment de-escalation in HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer?
options: These tumors respond poorly to standard therapy
Their high survival rates allow reducing long-term toxicity (fibrosis, dysphagia, osteoradionecrosis)
De-escalation is already the standard of care
items.explanation: Because advanced HPV-related oropharyngeal tumors have high survival with combined-modality therapy, de-escalation aims to decrease long-term morbidity (neck fibrosis, swallowing problems, osteoradionecrosis). It is not yet standard — guidelines currently treat HPV-positive and carcinogen-induced tumors identically.
items.reference: TREATMENT APPROACHES FOR HPV-RELATED HEAD AND NECK CANCERS: "de-escalation protocols have attracted widespread interest. The goal here is to decrease the long-term morbidity resulting from high-dose radiation therapy..."

items.statement: Over 50% of oropharyngeal tumors in the United States are caused by HPV.
items.explanation: True — over 50% of oropharyngeal tumors in the US are caused by HPV (higher in many urban centers), with HPV-16 the dominant subtype.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS: "Over 50% of oropharyngeal tumors are caused by HPV in the United States..."

items.statement: Retinoid supplements such as cis-retinoic acid prevent head and neck cancer in smokers.
items.explanation: False — retinoid supplements have not been shown to prevent head and neck (or lung) cancer and may increase the risk in active smokers.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS: "Supplements of retinoids, such as cis-retinoic acid, have not been shown to prevent head and neck cancers (or lung cancer) and may increase the risk in active smokers."

items.statement: CT screening for lung cancer is recommended in heavy smokers who have already developed a head and neck cancer.
items.explanation: True — because second primaries develop in the head/neck, lung, or esophagus from the same carcinogen exposure, CT lung-cancer screening is recommended in heavy smokers with a head and neck cancer.
items.reference: HISTOPATHOLOGY, CARCINOGENESIS, AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY: "CT screening for lung cancer in heavy smokers who have already developed a head and neck cancer is recommended."

items.statement: Overt distant metastases are present at initial diagnosis in fewer than 10% of head and neck cancer patients.
items.explanation: True — overt distant metastases are found in <10% of patients at diagnosis and are more common with advanced nodal stage; microscopic involvement (lungs, bones, liver) is more common.
items.reference: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: "Overt distant metastases are found in <10% of patients at initial diagnosis..."

items.statement: Overall 5-year survival for localized (stage I/II) head and neck cancer is 60–90%.
items.explanation: True — localized disease treated with curative intent (surgery or radiation) has an overall 5-year survival of 60–90%, with most recurrences occurring within 2 years and usually local.
items.reference: LOCALIZED DISEASE: "Overall 5-year survival is 60–90%."

items.statement: Locally or regionally advanced disease is the stage of presentation for more than 50% of head and neck cancer patients.
items.explanation: True — locally or regionally advanced disease (large primary and/or cervical node metastases) is the presenting stage for over 50% of patients.
items.reference: LOCALLY OR REGIONALLY ADVANCED DISEASE: "Such patients...is the stage of presentation for >50% of patients."

items.statement: Concomitant chemoradiotherapy causes less mucositis than radiation alone.
items.explanation: False — concomitant chemoradiotherapy increases toxicity, with grade 3 or 4 mucositis in 70–80% of patients, though it improves 5-year survival by about 8%.
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "Toxicity (especially mucositis, grade 3 or 4, in 70–80%) is increased with concomitant chemoradiotherapy."

items.statement: For advanced nasopharyngeal cancer, adding neoadjuvant chemotherapy before concomitant chemoradiotherapy has become standard of care.
items.explanation: True — neoadjuvant chemotherapy before concomitant chemoradiotherapy is standard for advanced nasopharyngeal cancer, with 5-year survival exceeding 80% in a Southeast Asian study.
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "the addition of neoadjuvant chemotherapy before concomitant chemoradiotherapy has been adopted as standard of care leading to 5-year survival rates exceeding 80%..."

items.statement: Adding cetuximab to standard chemoradiotherapy regimens further improves survival and is recommended.
items.explanation: False — adding cetuximab to standard chemoradiotherapy has failed to show further survival improvement and is not recommended (though cetuximab does improve survival versus radiotherapy alone for cisplatin-ineligible patients).
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "The addition of cetuximab to standard chemoradiotherapy regimens has failed to show further improvement in survival and is not recommended."

items.statement: Treatment guidelines currently recommend de-escalated therapy for all HPV-related head and neck cancers.
items.explanation: False — at present, treatment guidelines for HPV-related tumors are identical to carcinogen-induced tumors; de-escalation approaches remain investigational pending validation by clinical trials.
items.reference: TREATMENT APPROACHES FOR HPV-RELATED HEAD AND NECK CANCERS: "At present, treatment guidelines for HPV-related tumors are identical to carcinogen-induced tumors."

items.statement: Between 30% and 50% of patients with locoregionally advanced head and neck cancer experience recurrence.
items.explanation: True — 30–50% of patients with locoregionally advanced disease experience recurrence (frequently outside the head and neck), and 5–10% present with metastatic disease.
items.reference: RECURRENT AND/OR METASTATIC DISEASE: "30–50% of patients with locoregionally advanced disease experience recurrence, frequently outside the head and neck region."

items.statement: Specific environmental carcinogens have been clearly identified as causes of salivary gland tumors.
items.explanation: False — no specific risk factors or environmental carcinogens have been identified for salivary gland tumors, unlike the strong alcohol/tobacco association of mucosal squamous cancers.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS: "No specific risk factors or environmental carcinogens have been identified for salivary gland tumors."

items.assertion: Heavy smokers treated for an early head and neck cancer should undergo CT screening for lung cancer.
items.reason: Second primaries in these patients reflect the same carcinogen exposure and commonly arise in the lung or esophagus.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because field carcinogenesis produces second primaries (lung, esophagus, head/neck), lung CT screening is recommended in these heavy smokers.
items.reference: HISTOPATHOLOGY, CARCINOGENESIS, AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY: "These second primaries develop in the head and neck area, the lung, or the esophagus."

items.assertion: A persistent head and neck lesion that fails to resolve on antibiotics warrants prompt further workup.
items.reason: Continuing antibiotic treatment for such a lesion may forfeit the chance for early diagnosis of a malignancy.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because a non-resolving lesion may be cancer, persisting with antibiotics risks delayed diagnosis, so further workup is indicated.
items.reference: CLINICAL PRESENTATION: "to simply continue the antibiotic treatment may be to lose the chance of early diagnosis of a malignancy."

items.assertion: HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer has a separate AJCC staging system from carcinogen-induced tumors.
items.reason: HPV-related oropharyngeal malignancies consistently have a more favorable prognosis.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because HPV-positive tumors have a better outlook (advanced nodal disease can still be early-stage equivalent), the AJCC 8th edition created a separate staging system.
items.reference: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: "HPV-related oropharyngeal malignancies have consistently been shown to have a better prognosis, and in the eighth edition of the AJCC staging manual, a separate staging system...has been included."

items.assertion: Radiation therapy is often chosen over surgery for early laryngeal cancer.
items.reason: Radiation therapy preserves voice function in laryngeal cancer.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: radiation is preferred for larynx cancer specifically to preserve voice function (whereas surgery is preferred for small oral-cavity lesions to avoid radiation toxicity).
items.reference: LOCALIZED DISEASE: "Radiation therapy is often preferred for laryngeal cancer to preserve voice function..."

items.assertion: Concomitant chemoradiotherapy is a conceptually attractive approach for bulky head and neck tumors.
items.reason: Head and neck recurrences develop most commonly locoregionally, and concomitant chemotherapy enhances radiation tumor-cell killing.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because recurrences are mainly local and concurrent chemotherapy radiosensitizes the tumor, concomitant chemoradiotherapy is well-suited to bulky disease.
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "The concomitant approach is aimed at enhancing tumor cell killing by radiation therapy in the presence of chemotherapy (radiation enhancement) and is a conceptually attractive approach for bulky tumors."

items.assertion: Cetuximab should replace cisplatin during chemoradiotherapy for HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer to reduce toxicity.
items.reason: Substituting cetuximab for cisplatin in this population yielded equivalent survival with fewer side effects.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Trials substituting cetuximab for cisplatin in HPV-related cancer showed INFERIOR survival, so cisplatin remains standard. Both false = option 4.
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "trials substituting cisplatin with the EGFR inhibitor cetuximab in that patient population have shown inferior survival."

items.assertion: EGFR overexpression in head and neck cancer is a reliable predictor of response to EGFR inhibitors.
items.reason: EGFR inhibitors are active as single agents in only about 10–15% of patients.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — EGFR overexpression is not a good predictor of response. The reason is true (low single-agent activity) and explains why the assertion fails. False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: HISTOPATHOLOGY, CARCINOGENESIS, AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY: "it has not been to be a good predictor of tumor response to EGFR inhibitors, which are active in only about 10–15% of patients as single agents."

items.assertion: Treatment de-escalation is being studied for HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer.
items.reason: These tumors have high survival rates, making reduction of long-term radiation morbidity (fibrosis, dysphagia, osteoradionecrosis) a worthwhile goal.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because outcomes are excellent, de-escalation aims to lower the long-term toxicity of high-dose radiation while preserving cure.
items.reference: TREATMENT APPROACHES FOR HPV-RELATED HEAD AND NECK CANCERS: "The goal here is to decrease the long-term morbidity resulting from high-dose radiation therapy, including extensive neck fibrosis, swallowing problems, and osteoradonecrosis of the jaw."

items.assertion: Postoperative chemoradiotherapy (rather than radiation alone) is given when there is extracapsular nodal spread or positive surgical margins.
items.reason: Concomitant chemoradiotherapy significantly improves outcomes over postoperative radiation alone in tumors with these high-risk features.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because chemoradiotherapy outperforms radiation alone specifically in high-risk pathology, those features (extracapsular spread, positive margins) prompt the addition of chemotherapy.
items.reference: Concomitant Chemoradiotherapy: "Concomitant chemoradiotherapy produces a significant improvement over postoperative radiation therapy alone for patients whose tumors demonstrate higher risk features..."

items.assertion: Induction chemotherapy can allow organ preservation in laryngeal and hypopharyngeal cancer.
items.reason: Most patients receiving three cycles show tumor reduction, with clinically complete responses in up to half.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because induction chemotherapy produces high response rates, responders can avoid laryngectomy and preserve the organ.
items.reference: Induction Chemotherapy: "This 'sequential' multimodality therapy allows for organ preservation in patients with laryngeal and hypopharyngeal cancer and results in higher cure rates compared with radiotherapy alone."
